text: Solar cells incorporating alkyl ammonium metal halides as light absorbers offer a compelling blend of high power conversion efficiency and simplified fabrication, positioning them as a leading candidate for cost-effective thin-film photovoltaics. Despite their potential, these devices typically suffer from significant degradation under prolonged solar exposure. In this work, we demonstrate that this degradation is quasi-reversible and can be substantially mitigated by optimizing the solar cell’s operating environment. Through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) conducted on devices featuring methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), and mixed-cation $MA_xFA_{1-x}$ lead triiodide active layers, we identify distinct kinetic behaviors. Variable-temperature EIS reveals that the diffusion coefficient of MA ions is significantly higher than that of FA ions. Furthermore, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis of $MAPbI_3$ indicates a structural phase transition and lattice expansion at standard operating temperatures. Based on these findings, we propose that the accelerated degradation of $MAPbI_3$ under sunlight is driven by thermally activated ion transport coupled with a lattice-expanding phase transition—both of which are triggered by the infrared component of the solar spectrum. By applying these insights, we demonstrate that device stability and operational lifetimes are dramatically enhanced when using white-light LEDs, infrared-cutoff filters, or active cooling systems.
